dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, you have a report of a diabetic issue at 1100 block of Broad Street.
Hello sir, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 tonight?
I've had chest pain and shortness of breath for several hours.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your name? When is your birthday? What county are we in? What happened today?
My name is Tom, birthday is September 23rd. We're in Richmond. I was at home when I started having chest pain.
What's the date here? Do you know where you are right now? Who are you speaking with?
It is April 11th. I'm in my apartment. I'm talking to you, the emergency crew. I need help with my chest.
Your airway is open and you're speaking clearly. We need to evaluate a bit further now.
Sir, your breathing is adequate, 18 per minute, but you seem a bit short of breath.
Let's check your pulse and blood pressure now.
Your pulse is 144, which is on the high side. We call that tachycardia.
Your respiratory rate is 18 and remains stable.
Your blood pressure is 195 over 90. That's high and we'll need to monitor it.
Oxygen saturation is 99%. That's normal.
I'm going to test your blood sugar now. You might feel a tiny pinch.
Your blood glucose is 463 mg/dL. We'll monitor this closely during our assessment.
Mr. Smith, can you tell us when the chest pain started and what you were doing then?
It started around 6 or 7 hours ago while I was just sitting in my apartment.
You mentioned you have a history of diabetes. Have you had episodes of high blood sugar before tonight?
Yes, in the past, but I didn't get it checked. I haven't had a functioning glucometer recently.
Can you describe the exact feeling of the pain? On a scale of 1 to 10, what would you say it is?
It's a pressure, centered in my chest, no radiation. I'd rate it a 7.
Are there other conditions or issues you'd like to tell us about?
I have high blood pressure, anxiety, depression, and high cholesterol. I take several medications for them.
Could you share what medications you're taking at the moment?
I take Depakote, Risperdal, Seroquel, Paxil, metformin, Glypizide, and Pravastatin.
Do you have any allergies to medications or other substances?
I'm allergic to Penicillin and Benadryl.
We now want to assess your heart rhythm and check for any signs of infarction. We'll use a 4-lead ECG for that.
The ECG shows a sinus tachycardia.
We've just obtained a 12-lead EKG. It confirms sinus tachycardia and shows signs of a posterior infarct.
We did a second 12-lead and it again shows tachycardia and STEMI changes. There are occasional ventricular premature complexes noted.
Given the EKG and your symptoms, we are activating the STEMI protocol.
I need to start an IV in your left arm. You might feel a small pinch when we place it.
The IV was placed successfully and flushed. We've now got a secure line for medication delivery.
We're administering 324 milligrams of aspirin now. We've verified this aligns with standing orders for chest pain and STEMI.
I'll record a second set of vitals. Pulse 133, blood pressure at 204 over 102, and O2 at 100%.
I'll now record a follow-up EKG to evaluate for any changes from the first one.
The 12-lead EKG still shows tachycardia and STEMI changes. There are no new developments at this time.
We plan to take you straight to VCU Health for further cardiac care. That sound okay with you, Mr. Smith?
Yeah, that's the closest emergency room. Proceed.
We'll be placing you onto the stretcher now. Can you assist by holding onto the straps?
Yes, I can manage that.
We're also going to give you a 0.4 mg Nitroglycerin sublingual tablet now. It should help ease some of the pressure.
Mr. Smith, do you feel any improvement in the chest pain after the medication?
A little, it's now down from 7 to about a 5.
Pulse is 133, blood pressure is 210 over 84, and oxygen is 98%.
Any new or worsening symptoms or side effects now?
No, only mild chest discomfort.
Recent alcohol or illicit substance use prior to your symptoms?
No, I haven't used either.
You mentioned non-insulin dependent diabetes. Tell me, how long have you had it, and how do you manage it?
A few years. Mostly through lifestyle and medications.
We're ready for transport now. The hospital has been advised we're on the way. Any final questions for us?
No, I think that's all.
We're moving under protocol 2-2 for ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Hospital has been informed accordingly.
We've done one final vitals check, and everything seems stable enough for transport.
Pulse is 133, BP is 178 over 92, and oxygen is 97%.
Mr. Smith is in a semi-Fowler's position, awake, and stable. I'm all set for the move.
A third EKG has been obtained at the destination and still shows tachycardia, STEMI changes, and signs of a possible posterior infarct.
The EKG also reveals a potential atrial flutter. We'll make sure this finding is communicated to the hospital.
Pain remains at a 5 when we arrived at the hospital. That's an improvement from earlier.
